name: Austin Abundance Project
tagline: Remove the barriers. Let people build families.
mission:
Society should make it radically easy for people who want children to have them. The barriers to family formation — unaffordable childcare, scarce family-sized housing, nonexistent parental leave, crumbling school infrastructure — are policy failures, not personal failures. Fix the policy, and people who want families can build them.

This is not about pressuring anyone to have children or moralizing about birth rates. It is about removing structural obstacles for the people who already want kids but cannot afford them or cannot make the logistics work. And it is emphatically not about traditional family structures exclusively — single parents, same-sex couples, non-traditional families all face the same infrastructure failures, and the solutions should serve everyone.

We treat childcare, parental leave, family-sized housing, and pediatric healthcare as INFRASTRUCTURE problems with engineering solutions, not culture war issues.

The accelerationist case is simple: more humans means more ideas means more progress. A society that makes parenthood punishing is leaving human potential on the table. Austin's tech culture should understand this intuitively — we optimize systems for a living. Family formation is a system with fixable bottlenecks.

Why Austin, specifically? Because the bottlenecks are acute here:

• Austin has one of the worst childcare cost-to-income ratios in Texas. Average infant care costs $12,000-15,000 per year. Waitlists are months long. Large swaths of the city are childcare deserts with no licensed providers within reasonable distance.

• Travis County Commissioners Court just allocated $5,046,004 to childcare quality improvement and capacity expansion through an interlocal agreement with the City of Austin, from the Raising Travis County fund. This is real money moving — it needs organized advocates to ensure it is spent effectively.

• Austin's median age is approximately 34. The city is full of millennials and young Gen-X in peak family-formation years, many working in tech with the income but facing structural barriers: housing cost, childcare scarcity, no paid leave, commute times eating into family hours.

• Texas has NO state-mandated paid family leave. Federal FMLA only guarantees unpaid leave. The fight happens at the employer and city level — Austin can lead as a tech hub where major employers are persuadable.

• The housing crisis IS a family formation crisis. You cannot have a second kid in a one-bedroom apartment. Family-sized housing (2BR+) is the scarcest and most expensive segment of Austin's market. Building more housing IS pro-family policy.

• Austin ISD faces enrollment decline and budget pressures as families with resources leave for suburbs with newer schools. Keeping families in urban Austin requires both housing affordability and school quality.
theory_of_change: Family formation barriers in Austin are concentrated in a few specific, addressable policy domains: childcare facility zoning, employer benefits transparency, housing supply, and county/city investment oversight. The $5M Raising Travis County investment creates an immediate accountability opportunity. Employer transparency campaigns work because Austin's tech labor market is competitive — companies respond to talent retention pressure. Childcare zoning reform is achievable because it aligns with both progressive (equity) and conservative (deregulation) values. And the housing connection means we amplify the YIMBY movement's work while adding a constituency — parents and prospective parents — that politicians find very sympathetic. Every campaign has a specific lever, a specific decision-maker, and a realistic path to impact.

political_positioning.description: Center-left economically — we believe in public investment in childcare and family infrastructure. Libertarian-leaning socially — we do not moralize about family structure and support all family types. Explicitly not aligned with religious-right pro-natalism. Explicitly not aligned with anti-tech or anti-growth perspectives. We are a broad coalition: tech workers, working parents, childcare providers, urbanists who see the housing-family connection, and employers who compete for talent and understand that family-friendly policy is a competitive advantage.

key_policy_positions: Reform childcare facility zoning to make it easier to open home-based and small-center childcare in residential areas
Monitor and advocate for effective deployment of the $5M Raising Travis County childcare investment
Advocate for employer childcare benefits and paid parental leave at Austin's major tech employers
Push for family-sized unit requirements or incentives (2BR+) in new housing developments
Require paid parental leave disclosures from City of Austin contractors
Advocate for competitive paid parental leave policies at the City of Austin, Travis County, and AISD as Austin's largest public employers
Research and publish employer transparency data on parental leave and family benefits
Support co-housing and innovative family housing models
Advocate for urban school quality to keep families in Austin rather than losing them to suburban flight
Connect family housing needs to the broader housing supply agenda

active_campaigns.title: Childcare Desert Mapping & Advocacy
active_campaigns.summary:
⚡ UPDATE (May 17, 2026): MAJOR STATE DEVELOPMENT — Texas House passed HB 500 with $100 MILLION for child care scholarships. The funding allows the Texas Workforce Commission to serve additional families — 95,000 families are currently on the waiting list. Governor Abbott also signed HB 117 (streamlines childcare regulations) and HB 4903 (creates the Quad-Agency Childcare Initiative Commission). This creates a rare alignment of city ($5M Raising Travis County), county, and state ($100M) childcare funding.

⚡ ZONING CONNECTION: Council voted 9-1 on May 8 to push the HOME Initiative deeper into single-family neighborhoods — loosening lot-size, setback, and subdivision rules. This directly connects to childcare facility zoning: if residential lots can now accommodate duplexes and townhomes, they should also be able to accommodate home-based childcare facilities. Over 600 HOME applications approved, adding 1,200+ units. The childcare desert mapping project should overlay HOME Phase 2 approval locations to identify where new housing density creates new childcare demand.

CORE CAMPAIGN: Use public data to map Austin's childcare deserts — areas with no licensed childcare providers within reasonable distance. Three specific data sources form the foundation: the Texas HHS licensed childcare facility database (provider locations, capacity, license status), Census ACS data on children under 5 by tract (demand side), and Travis County childcare subsidy utilization data showing where subsidized families live versus where they can actually use their subsidies (access gaps). Overlay these three layers to produce an actual map of where the gaps are — this is a data project, exactly the kind of thing an AI-assisted civic org can do better than traditional advocacy groups.

Simultaneously, monitor the $5,046,004 Raising Travis County childcare investment AND the new $100M state scholarship program with facility-level and neighborhood-level accountability. Track which specific facilities get funded, which neighborhoods receive investment, and what the outcome metrics are — new slots created, waitlist reduction, subsidy utilization rates. Are new state scholarships reaching Austin families? Are new slots being created in childcare deserts?

Advocate for childcare facility zoning reform — current rules make it unnecessarily difficult to open home-based and small-center childcare in residential areas. This is a deregulatory reform that should appeal across the political spectrum.

⚡ MAY 23 UPDATE: Council approved the citywide Density Bonus Program on May 22, expanding housing production tools. Combined with the Light Rail Transit Overlay (May 16) and HOME Initiative, Austin now has a comprehensive transit-oriented development framework that will shape where new families live — and where new childcare capacity is needed. The Dog's Head annexation (2,600 acres, approved May 22) will eventually create entirely new neighborhoods requiring childcare infrastructure from scratch. The childcare desert mapping project should incorporate both DBC-eligible areas and the Dog's Head development zone as future demand hotspots. ⚡ Texas House HB 500 ($100M childcare scholarships) + $5M Raising Travis County creates a rare multi-level funding alignment — track how state scholarships flow to Austin families.

**May 24 update:** BEATF bond presentation May 28 (4 DAYS). Pct 4 runoff Election Day May 26 (2 DAYS) — the winner influences county-level childcare funding. AISD board final vote on $181M deficit budget June 18 (25 DAYS) — the budget directly impacts school quality and family retention.

**May 25 update:** ⚡ Pct 4 runoff TOMORROW (May 26) — the winner directly influences county-level childcare funding through the $5M+ Raising Travis County investment. ⚡ KUT reported (May 11) that the Raising Travis County program is deploying: families won't pay more than 7% of annual income on childcare, with 1,000 scholarships expected by October. $2.6M has already been distributed to 150 daycare centers for operations and staff improvements. Council also approved zoning changes allowing more properties to be zoned for day care services. ⚡ AISD budget draft DELAYED to June 4 — Superintendent Segura identified $130M in cuts but needs ~$50M more. The $181M deficit and 215 FTE educator cuts remain the baseline. Board must pass budget by end of June. BEATF bond presentation May 28 (3 DAYS).

**May 26 update:** ⚡ Pct 4 runoff is TODAY — the winner directly influences county-level childcare funding through the $5M+ Raising Travis County investment. BEATF bond presentation May 28 (2 DAYS). AISD budget draft now expected June 4 (9 DAYS). The $181M deficit and 215 FTE educator cuts continue to shape the family retention conversation.

**May 27 update:** ⚡ Pct 4 runoff results: George Morales won ~55% — as the new commissioner, he will influence county-level childcare funding including the $5M+ Raising Travis County investment. HD-49: Montserrat Garibay won ~60% over former CM Kathie Tovo, running pro-education and anti-voucher — a strong signal for family-oriented state policy. TOMORROW (May 28) council agenda: BEATF bond presentation (staff recommend delay to 2028; CMs Ellis & Laine favor ~$350M smaller bond), Downtown Density Bonus hearing (DDB400/DDB850), I-35 Cap & Stitch update creating economic opportunity corridors, housing tax credits expanding affordable family housing pipeline, and rental fee disclosure.

⚡ MAY 28 UPDATE: Council agenda today includes BEATF bond presentation ($766.5M package with $200M housing), housing tax credit support resolution, and rental fee disclosure ordinance. AISD budget draft still delayed to June 4 (7 DAYS) — $181M deficit, 215 FTE educator cuts. Superintendent has identified $152.7M in potential cuts but needs ~$50M more. Board final vote June 18 (21 DAYS). George Morales (new Pct 4 commissioner) will influence county-level childcare funding including $5M+ Raising Travis County investment.

**May 29 update:** ⚡ MAJOR FUNDING UPDATE: Travis County Commissioners approved $17.65M in new contracts to expand child care, after-school, and summer programs — serving more than 5,200 children. This is part of the $75M ANNUAL Raising Travis County voter-approved fund (Proposition A, November 2024), far exceeding the earlier $5M figure. Under the scholarship program, families won't pay more than 7% of annual income on childcare — down from the current average of 15%+ of median household income. 1,000 scholarships expected by October. $2.6M already distributed to 150 daycare centers for operations and staff improvements. This is the fastest implementation of any initiative of its kind in the country and the first of its kind in Texas. ⚡ BOND DECISION DELAYED: Council pushed the bond vote to July. Mayor Watson opposes a 2026 bond entirely. The full $766.5M BEATF package (includes $200M housing) may be dead — the ~$350M parks-focused alternative excludes housing. Housing advocates must mobilize before July. ⚡ AISD budget draft still delayed — expected June 4 (6 DAYS), final board vote June 18 (20 DAYS). The $181M deficit and 215 FTE educator cuts continue. Superintendent identified $152.7M in potential cuts but needs ~$50M more.

**May 30 update:** ⚡ BOND PROCEEDS WITHOUT HOUSING: Council voted 6-2-1 on May 29 to proceed with ~$390M bond development (parks, transportation, community facilities) — overruling Watson's opposition. Housing ($200M) and public facilities ($131.1M) are NOT in the current direction. The July 23 final vote is the advocacy window for adding family infrastructure back. ⚡ AISD budget draft expected June 4 (5 DAYS) — the ~$50M gap between identified cuts ($152.7M) and the full $181M deficit remains unresolved. Board final vote June 18 (19 DAYS). The Raising Travis County program ($17.65M, 5,200 children) continues as the strongest counterweight to the AISD cuts — county-level childcare investment provides a stability anchor for families navigating school budget turmoil.

**May 31 update:** AISD budget proposal expected June 4 (4 DAYS) — librarian-splitting across campuses is the latest cut. Board final vote June 18 (18 DAYS). The $181M deficit and 215 FTE educator cuts continue to drive family suburban flight, compounding the childcare demand problem: fewer families staying in urban Austin means lower childcare utilization, threatening provider viability. The Raising Travis County program ($17.65M, serving 5,200 children, families capped at 7% of income) remains the strongest counter-force — track scholarship distribution to childcare desert zones. State HB 500 ($100M childcare scholarships, 95,000 families on waitlist) creates the rare city-county-state funding alignment. Bond final vote July 23 (53 DAYS) — housing ($200M) excluded from current ~$390M direction; advocacy window open.

**June 1 update:** ⚡ RAISING TRAVIS COUNTY SCALING RAPIDLY: Nearly 300 kids have received childcare scholarships, with 1,000 expected by October. Program has awarded $28M+ total including $17.34M to Workforce Solutions Capital Area for scholarships and $4.16M for quality improvements at 150 providers. Virtual town hall June 16 at 6 PM. Additional $17M in childcare contracts coming before Commissioners Court. George Morales (new Pct 4 commissioner) now seated and voting on these contracts. AISD budget proposal expected June 4 (3 DAYS). Bond final vote July 23 (52 DAYS) — housing ($200M) still excluded from ~$390M direction.

**June 3 update:** Council approved sweeping development reforms in late May: DBC density bonus (May 22), Dog’s Head 2,600-acre annexation (May 22), minimum lot size reduction from 5,750 to 1,800 sq ft (May 26), and new missing middle/mixed-use zoning districts. The ~$390M bond (parks/transportation, final vote July 23) and $156.3M Certificates of Obligation (fire stations, public safety) advance infrastructure investment. Council in summer recess until July 23. AISD FY27 budget ($181M deficit) creates both fiscal headwinds and housing production incentives.

**June 4 update:** Raising Travis County on track for fastest implementation in the country. Children 3 and under no longer waiting for scholarships by end of summer (KUT). Morales appointment June 11 — first new Pct 4 commissioner in 31 years. AISD budget proposal: 558 positions, 11 school closures — childcare demand may shift as families reassess school options.

**June 5 update:** Raising Travis County virtual town hall scheduled June 16 at 6 PM (11 DAYS) — opportunity to ask about scholarship distribution patterns and childcare desert coverage. Nearly 300 kids have received scholarships, 1,000 expected by October. $17.65M in contracts serving 5,200+ children through 12 community organizations. AISD budget vote June 18 (13 DAYS) — 558 positions and 11 school closures will reshape family geography and childcare demand patterns. George Morales formally appointed Pct 4 commissioner June 11 (6 DAYS). Bond final vote July 23 — housing ($200M) still excluded.

**June 7 update:** AISD budget crisis deepens: 558 positions cut, 11 schools closing this summer. Parents/teachers protested June 5. Board vote June 18 (11 DAYS). School closures directly impact family access to childcare, after-school programs, and community services. The district is exploring selling 4 properties ($60M) — potential sites for family-oriented redevelopment. Bond: BEATF proposed $766.5M and $436M options. Council reviewing. 2/3 of residents support tax increase.

**June 8 update:** Raising Travis County virtual town hall June 16 at 6 PM — 8 DAYS AWAY. Nearly 300 kids have received scholarships, 1,000 expected by October. Wait times dropped from 2 years to months (KUT). Children 3 and under will no longer wait for scholarships by end of summer. George Morales formally appointed Pct 4 commissioner June 11 (3 DAYS) — he will immediately begin voting on childcare contracts. AISD board vote June 18 (10 DAYS) — new transportation hub model proposed alongside 558 position cuts and 11 school closures. School closures directly impact family childcare and after-school program access.

**June 9 update:** ⚡ MAJOR MILESTONE: Raising Travis County has now funded scholarships for 1,000 children — reaching the October target months ahead of schedule. Over 2,650 students in out-of-school time programs. $2.6M in gap funding distributed to 150 providers. This is the fastest implementation of any voter-approved children's fund in the country — and the first in Texas. Virtual town hall June 16 at 6 PM (7 DAYS). George Morales appointed Pct 4 commissioner June 11 (2 DAYS) — immediately begins voting on additional $17M in childcare contracts. AISD board vote June 18 (9 DAYS) — school closures directly impact family childcare and after-school access.

**June 10 update:** Raising Travis County at record pace — 1,000 scholarships funded, October target reached months early. Wait times dropped from 2 years to months. $2.6M gap funding to 150 providers. Virtual town hall June 16 at 6 PM (6 DAYS). George Morales sworn in as Pct 4 commissioner TOMORROW (June 11) — immediately begins voting on additional childcare contracts. CDBG public comment period opens June 17. AISD full cut plan released June 3: 215 educator salaries, school closures impacting family childcare and after-school access. Board vote June 18 (8 DAYS).

**June 11 update:** ⚡ George Morales sworn in as Pct 4 commissioner TODAY — immediately begins voting on Raising Travis County childcare contracts. Program at record pace: 1,000+ scholarships funded, wait times dropped from 2 years to months, $2.6M gap funding to 150 providers. Virtual town hall June 16 at 6 PM (5 DAYS). CDBG public comment period opens June 17 (6 DAYS) through July 20, public hearing July 14. AISD board vote June 18 (7 DAYS) — school closures impact family childcare and after-school access. Travis County offices closed June 18-19 for Juneteenth.

**June 12 update:** Morales's first full day as Pct 4 commissioner — immediately voting on Raising Travis County childcare contracts. His priorities include expanded bus service (CapMetro coordination) and affordable healthcare (Central Health). Program at record pace: 1,000+ scholarships, wait times dropped from 2 years to months. Virtual town hall June 16 at 6 PM (4 DAYS). CDBG public comment opens June 17 (5 DAYS). ⚡ AISD deficit ballooned to $95M — school closures impact childcare and after-school access; district needs September loan. Travis County offices closed June 18-19 for Juneteenth.

**June 13 update:** Commissioners Court approved $17.65M expansion of Raising Travis County childcare and after-school programs — 1,000+ scholarships annually for children up to 3. Morales now serving as Pct 4 commissioner, immediately voting on these contracts. Virtual town hall June 16 at 6 PM (3 DAYS). CDBG public comment opens June 17 (4 DAYS). AISD board vote on $181M shortfall June 18 (5 DAYS) — school closures impact childcare and after-school access. Travis County offices closed June 18-19 for Juneteenth.

**June 14 update:** Raising Travis County virtual town hall June 16 at 6 PM — 2 DAYS. $17.65M expansion approved; 1,000+ scholarships annually for children up to 3. Morales serving as Pct 4 commissioner, voting on these contracts in his first week. CDBG public comment opens June 17 (3 DAYS). AISD board vote on $181M shortfall June 18 (4 DAYS) but may delay to June 25 — school closures impact childcare access. Travis County offices closed June 18-19 for Juneteenth.

**June 15 update:** Raising Travis County virtual town hall TOMORROW (June 16) at 6 PM. $17.65M expansion approved; 1,000+ scholarships annually; wait times down from 2 years to months. CDBG public comment opens June 17 (2 DAYS). AISD board vote on $181M shortfall June 18 (3 DAYS) — school closures impact childcare access. Travis County offices closed June 18-19 for Juneteenth.

**June 16 update:** ⚡ Raising Travis County virtual town hall TODAY at 6 PM. $17.65M expansion approved; nearly 300 kids have received scholarships, target 1,000 by October; wait times down from 2 years to months — fastest rollout of any initiative of its kind in the country. CDBG public comment opens TOMORROW (June 17) through July 20. AISD board vote on $181M shortfall June 18 (2 DAYS). Travis County offices closed June 18-19 for Juneteenth.

**June 17 update:** Raising Travis County virtual town hall held yesterday (June 16). $17.65M expansion; nearly 300 kids have received scholarships, target 1,000 by October; wait times down from 2 years to months. CDBG public comment period opens TODAY (June 17) through July 20 — draft PY26 Action Plan available at traviscountytx.gov. Public hearing July 14 at 9 AM. AISD board vote TOMORROW (June 18) — school closures directly impact childcare access. Travis County offices CLOSED June 18-19 for Juneteenth.

**June 18 update:** Travis County offices CLOSED today and tomorrow for Juneteenth — 37th Annual celebration at 800 Lavaca 11 AM-2 PM. $17.65M childcare expansion on track; nearly 300 kids have received scholarships, target 1,000 by October. CDBG public comment continues through July 20 — draft PY26 Action Plan and ConPlan/PY24-25 amendments all open for comment. Public hearing July 14 at 9 AM. ⚡ AISD board voting TODAY on $181M shortfall — school closures directly impact childcare access and family stability.

**June 19 update:** Juneteenth — Travis County offices CLOSED. ⚡ AISD budget approved 7-1 at $205M — 10 school closures directly impact childcare access and family stability. Last-minute amendment preserved full-time librarians at all campuses. $17.65M Raising Travis County expansion on track; nearly 300 scholarships issued, target 1,000 by October. CDBG public comment through July 20.

**June 20 update:** $17.65M Raising Travis County expansion on track — nearly 300 scholarships issued, target 1,000 by October. Wait times dropped from 2 years to months. AISD boundary realignment workshops June 22-23 — school closures directly impact childcare access patterns across affected neighborhoods. CDBG public comment through July 20; hearing July 14 at 9 AM.

**June 21 update:** ⚡ $51.7M already distributed through Raising Travis County initiative. Nearly 300 scholarships issued, target 1,000 by October. Families pay ≤7% of income; eligible up to 85% SMI (~$92K for family of four). AISD boundary workshops TOMORROW: June 22 and 23 — school closures impact childcare access. CDBG public comment through July 20; hearing July 14 at 9 AM.

**June 22 update:** ⚡ AISD boundary workshop TODAY (10-11:30 AM virtual); second session tomorrow June 23 (6-7:30 PM). Two draft maps — "Oak" and "Elm" — show different elementary-to-middle-to-high school pathways after 10 school closures. Online comment card open through July 31; revised draft for trustees in August; board vote September; implementation August 2027. School closures reshape family childcare access patterns across affected neighborhoods — the childcare desert map should overlay boundary changes to identify new access gaps. $51.7M distributed through Raising Travis County (25 contracts, 9,402 children/youth served); demand still outpaces supply for nontraditional hours. CDBG public comment through July 20; hearing July 14 at 9 AM (22 DAYS). Bond survey closes TOMORROW — housing ($200M) still excluded from ~$390M direction. Council on recess until July 23 (31 DAYS).

June 23 update: Raising Travis County initiative continues scaling — $51.7M distributed across 25 contracts serving 9,402 children/youth. 1,000+ scholarships funded months ahead of October target. Texas HB 500 ($100M state scholarships) passed with 95,000 families on waiting lists. AISD budget approved at $205M — 558 positions cut, 10 school closures reshape childcare landscape. Bond survey closed today; council returns July 23 (30 DAYS).

**June 24 update:** Raising Travis County at $51.7M+ distributed — 25 contracts serving 9,402 children/youth. Nearly 300 scholarships issued, target 1,000 by October. Families pay ≤7% of income. AISD boundary workshops completed June 22-23 — school closures reshape childcare access patterns; comment card open through July 31. CDBG public comment through July 20; hearing July 14 at 9 AM (20 DAYS). Bond survey closed — housing ($200M) excluded.

**June 25 update:** Raising Travis County at $51.7M+ distributed — 25 contracts serving 9,402 children/youth. Nearly 300 scholarships issued, target 1,000 by October. Families pay ≤7% of income. Infant/toddler care and nontraditional-hours coverage remain key gaps. CDBG public comment through July 20; hearing July 14 at 9 AM (19 DAYS). Bond debate: housing ($200M) excluded from ~$400M direction.

**June 26 update:** ⚡ $17.65M childcare expansion approved — 25 contracts serving 5,200+ children, 180 providers supported. Nearly 300 scholarships issued, target 1,000 by October. Wait times cut from 2 years to months. Families pay ≤7% of income. Infant/toddler care and nontraditional-hours coverage remain key gaps. CDBG comment through July 20; hearing July 14 (18 DAYS). Bond: housing ($200M) excluded from ~$400M direction.

**June 27 update:** Raising Travis County: $75M voter-approved program scaling — $28M+ awarded total. $17.34M to Workforce Solutions for 1,000 annual scholarships. AISD opened 9 Apple Blossom Centers for extended after-school care. Nearly 300 scholarships issued, target 1,000 by October. CDBG comment through July 20; hearing July 14 (17 DAYS). ⚡ AISD boundary realignment POSTPONED to 2028-29 — Phase 1 draft August 7, more closures possible in Phase 2.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: Data-driven advocacy works because it makes the invisible visible. Council members and county commissioners can ignore abstract complaints about childcare scarcity but cannot ignore a map showing their district is a childcare desert. The $5M county investment creates an accountability hook — public money requires public scrutiny. Zoning reform for childcare facilities is achievable because it aligns with both left (equity, access) and right (deregulation, property rights) values, making it a rare bipartisan opportunity.
next_actions: Compile licensed childcare provider locations from Texas HHS database (provider addresses, capacity, license type)
Pull Census ACS data on children under 5 by tract for Travis County (demand-side demographics)
Obtain Travis County childcare subsidy utilization data: where subsidized families live vs. where they access care
Overlay all three data sources to produce childcare desert map identifying gap zones
Track $5M Raising Travis County fund: which facilities get funded, which neighborhoods, what outcome metrics
Draft childcare zoning reform proposal for residential areas
Present desert map and fund accountability findings to Travis County Commissioners Court

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of Childcare Desert Mapping & Advocacy often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Define clear outcomes, timelines, and guardrails before scaling spending or mandates.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Campaigns with published milestones and public reporting earn broader support and allow mid-course corrections.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "If Childcare Desert Mapping & Advocacy is worth doing, it is worth measuring with transparent benchmarks so everyone can see what is working."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Implement change in ways that reduce disruption and share benefits across existing residents and newcomers.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Phased implementation and regular public feedback often lower backlash while improving policy durability.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "We can move Childcare Desert Mapping & Advocacy forward while still listening to neighborhood-level concerns and adjusting implementation details."

common_ground: Public policy should solve real problems, not just signal values.
Residents deserve clear communication about tradeoffs.
Programs should be adjusted when evidence shows gaps.
conversation_traps_to_avoid: Assuming all critics are acting in bad faith.
Overpromising speed or certainty on complex implementation.
Using national partisan frames for local decisions.
when_to_acknowledge_uncertainty: When baseline data is incomplete or lagged.
When legal or budget decisions are still pending.
When implementation depends on partner agencies outside our control.
honest_limits: This campaign cannot alone fix every upstream structural problem.
We cannot guarantee identical outcomes in every neighborhood.
We rely on sustained public participation and institutional follow-through.

active_campaigns.title: Austin Family-Friendly Employer Index
active_campaigns.summary:
Research and publish a scorecard of Austin's top 25 tech employers — the ones competing for the same talent pool. Score on specific, comparable metrics: weeks of paid parental leave (birth and non-birth parent separately), childcare subsidy or on-site childcare, flexible work arrangements, and fertility/adoption benefits. Publish the scores. Name names.

The competitive pressure this creates is the whole theory of change — employers hate being publicly ranked below their talent competitors. In Austin's tight tech labor market, being publicly ranked as family-unfriendly is a recruiting liability. The index gives companies a reason to improve and gives workers information to make informed employment decisions.

Advocate for the City of Austin to require paid parental leave disclosures from city contractors — a lever the city actually has without state legislation.

**June 1 update:** $218M Austin Police Association contract demonstrates city-level employer leadership on compensation. APD recruitment up 166%. AT-Home Initiative ($6.7M, 5-year) proposals due June 2 — city and county partnering with Housing Connector and Zillow to expand housing access.

**June 4 update:** AISD budget cuts affect non-certified staff job security. District as major local employer facing 558 position reductions.

**June 5 update:** AISD budget vote June 18 (13 DAYS) — 558 positions affected. The district's treatment of affected staff through the transition is a case study in public employer practices under fiscal stress. Council on summer recess until July 23.

**June 22 update:** AISD budget approved at $205M — 558 positions eliminated, 10 school closures. Boundary workshops TODAY and tomorrow reshape employee assignments across campuses. Council on summer recess until July 23 (31 DAYS). Filing period for council races opens July 19 (27 DAYS) — new council members will influence FY2028 public employer benefit policies.

June 23 update: Campaign remains in planning phase. Bond survey closed today — results being compiled. AISD virtual boundary workshop tonight (6-7:30 PM). Council on recess until July 23 (30 DAYS).
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: Transparency creates competitive pressure in tight labor markets. When employer A's parental leave policy is publicly compared to employer B's, the laggard faces recruiting disadvantage. This works especially well in Austin's tech sector where talent competition is fierce and workers have options. The city contractor disclosure requirement adds a policy lever that doesn't require state legislation. Published rankings get media coverage, which amplifies the pressure beyond the direct readership.
next_actions: Identify Austin's top 25 tech employers by headcount — the companies competing for the same talent pool

Design scorecard with specific categories: weeks paid parental leave (birth parent), weeks paid parental leave (non-birth parent), childcare subsidy or on-site childcare, flexible work arrangements, fertility/adoption benefits
Send survey to HR departments with public records requests as backup
Research City of Austin contractor disclosure requirements and identify legal pathway
Draft op-ed framing the index as pro-business talent retention, not anti-employer
Partner with Austin tech community groups (Austin Tech Alliance, Capital Factory) for distribution

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of Austin Family-Friendly Employer Index often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Campaigns with published milestones and public reporting earn broader support and allow mid-course corrections.

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Implement change in ways that reduce disruption and share benefits across existing residents and newcomers.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Phased implementation and regular public feedback often lower backlash while improving policy durability.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "We can move Austin Family-Friendly Employer Index forward while still listening to neighborhood-level concerns and adjusting implementation details."

conversation_traps_to_avoid: Assuming all critics are acting in bad faith.
Overpromising speed or certainty on complex implementation.
Using national partisan frames for local decisions.
when_to_acknowledge_uncertainty: When baseline data is incomplete or lagged.
When legal or budget decisions are still pending.
When implementation depends on partner agencies outside our control.
honest_limits: This campaign cannot alone fix every upstream structural problem.
We cannot guarantee identical outcomes in every neighborhood.
We rely on sustained public participation and institutional follow-through.

active_campaigns.title: Family-Sized Housing Campaign
active_campaigns.summary:
Connect housing to family formation explicitly — and make this cross-reference bidirectional. The housing crisis IS a family formation crisis: you cannot have a second kid in a one-bedroom apartment. And the family formation crisis IS a housing crisis: when families leave Austin for the suburbs because they cannot find affordable 3-bedroom units, the city loses tax base, school enrollment drops, and the urban fabric frays.

Work with Austin YIMBY Action to ensure new development includes family-appropriate units — not just studios and 1-bedrooms. Family-sized housing (2BR+) is the scarcest and most expensive segment of Austin's market because developers maximize unit count at smaller sizes.

Advocate for family-sized unit incentives in new developments: density bonuses for projects that include a minimum percentage of 2BR+ units, reduced parking requirements for family units near transit, and support for co-housing and innovative family housing models. Every YIMBY win is a pro-family win. Every pro-family argument strengthens the YIMBY coalition. The YIMBY org's housing advocacy directly enables family formation — make this cross-reference explicit and bidirectional.

**June 1 update:** DBC density bonus program (approved May 22) now in implementation — developers can seek 15-60 feet additional height in exchange for 10% affordable units (80% MFI for sale, 50% MFI for rental). Bond final vote July 23 (52 DAYS) — housing ($200M) excluded from current ~$390M direction. Community bond survey showed two-thirds of residents willing to support tax increase; housing was a top priority. Housing advocates have a 52-day window to lobby for inclusion.

**June 4 update:** Development rules overhaul in implementation phase. Council on summer recess. Bond final vote July 23 (49 DAYS) — housing still excluded.

**June 5 update:** Bond 'summer standstill' continues. Housing ($200M) excluded from ~$390M bond direction. Final vote July 23 (48 DAYS). DBC implementation underway — monitor for family-sized unit (2BR+) inclusion in early developer applications. Council on summer recess.

**June 22 update:** Bond survey closes TOMORROW (June 23) — results so far: transportation (19.8%), housing/homelessness (18.5%), parks (16.3%) top priorities; ~70% of 2,002 respondents support tax increase. Housing ($200M) still excluded from ~$390M bond direction; final vote July 23 (31 DAYS). DBC in effect — monitor early applications for family-sized unit (2BR+) inclusion. AISD boundary workshops TODAY reshape school zones, influencing where families locate and where family-sized housing demand concentrates. Council on recess until July 23.

June 23 update: Bond survey CLOSED TODAY — housing $200M excluded from ~$390M direction. 53,000+ responses; transportation and housing tied as top priorities. AISD virtual boundary workshop TONIGHT (6-7:30 PM) — 'Oak' and 'Elm' maps reshape school pathways after 10 closures. DBC density bonus in effect since May 22. Council on recess until July 23 (30 DAYS). Bond final vote July 23.

**June 27 update:** ⚡ Dog's Head TIRZ: 12,000 housing units, 20% affordable, 260+ acres parkland — county TIRZ vote July 14 (17 DAYS). DBC: zero applications in 27 days. Bond: housing ($200M) excluded. AISD boundary realignment POSTPONED to 2028-29 — school consolidation reshapes family housing demand. Council on recess until July 23 (26 DAYS).
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: The housing-family connection is bidirectional: more housing enables family formation, and the family formation argument strengthens the pro-housing coalition. 'Build family-sized housing' is a more emotionally resonant message than 'increase housing supply' and reaches audiences who might not respond to urbanist framing. Parents and prospective parents are a powerful constituency that politicians find sympathetic. By working through the existing YIMBY coalition rather than creating parallel advocacy, we amplify rather than fragment the pro-building movement. Every rezoning hearing where parents testify about needing affordable family-sized units strengthens the case for density.
next_actions: Analyze Austin permitting data for unit size distribution in recent developments
Research density bonus programs in other cities that incentivize family-sized units
Draft family-sized housing incentive proposal for Austin Planning Commission
Coordinate with Austin YIMBY Action on joint advocacy and shared messaging
Identify co-housing and innovative family housing developers active in Texas
Develop family housing fact sheet: cost data, supply gaps, demographic need

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of Family-Sized Housing Campaign often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Define clear outcomes, timelines, and guardrails before scaling spending or mandates.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Campaigns with published milestones and public reporting earn broader support and allow mid-course corrections.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "If Family-Sized Housing Campaign is worth doing, it is worth measuring with transparent benchmarks so everyone can see what is working."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Implement change in ways that reduce disruption and share benefits across existing residents and newcomers.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Phased implementation and regular public feedback often lower backlash while improving policy durability.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "We can move Family-Sized Housing Campaign forward while still listening to neighborhood-level concerns and adjusting implementation details."

common_ground: Public policy should solve real problems, not just signal values.
Residents deserve clear communication about tradeoffs.
Programs should be adjusted when evidence shows gaps.
conversation_traps_to_avoid: Assuming all critics are acting in bad faith.
Overpromising speed or certainty on complex implementation.
Using national partisan frames for local decisions.
when_to_acknowledge_uncertainty: When baseline data is incomplete or lagged.
When legal or budget decisions are still pending.
When implementation depends on partner agencies outside our control.
honest_limits: This campaign cannot alone fix every upstream structural problem.
We cannot guarantee identical outcomes in every neighborhood.
We rely on sustained public participation and institutional follow-through.

active_campaigns.title: Parental Leave at City Hall
active_campaigns.summary:
Austin city government is itself a major employer. What is the City of Austin's parental leave policy? What about Travis County? AISD? These are public entities that the org can directly pressure through public comment and council advocacy — giving the org concrete local government leverage points similar to what the YIMBY org has with zoning and transit.

Research and publicize the parental leave policies of Austin's three largest public employers — the City of Austin, Travis County, and Austin ISD. Compare them to best-in-class municipal employers nationwide (Denver, Portland, Minneapolis, Seattle). Then advocate for improvements through the channels these entities are most responsive to: public comment periods, council resolutions, commissioners court testimony, and school board advocacy.

This is the most direct-action campaign in the portfolio. Unlike pressuring private employers through market mechanisms, public employers answer to elected officials who answer to voters. A small organized group showing up at council with a specific ask — 12 weeks paid leave for all city employees, for example — has a realistic path to a policy win.

**June 1 update:** Council voted 6-2-1 on May 29 to proceed with ~$390M bond. FY2027 budget conversations will include workforce retention — parental leave is a retention tool as APD (700 officers short), AISD (215 FTE cuts), and other departments face staffing challenges.

**June 4 update:** AISD budget proposal presented today reduces school police officers. 558 positions affected district-wide.

**June 5 update:** AISD budget vote June 18 (13 DAYS) — 558 positions affected including teachers, counselors, librarians, school police. The district's workforce policies during the transition are a direct test case for public employer family-friendliness under fiscal stress. Council on summer recess until July 23.

**June 19 update:** Juneteenth — city offices CLOSED. ⚡ AISD budget approved 7-1 at $205M — impacts AISD employees as a public employer. City and county parental leave policies under research.

**June 20 update:** AISD budget approved at $205M — impacts AISD employees as public employer; 558 positions eliminated. City and county parental leave policies under research. Council on recess until July 23.

**June 21 update:** AISD budget at $205M — 558 positions eliminated; impacts AISD employees as public employer. $51.7M distributed through Raising Travis County initiative. Council on recess until July 23 (32 DAYS).

**June 22 update:** AISD boundary workshops TODAY and tomorrow — 10 school closures trigger staff reassignments affecting employee working conditions. The district's treatment of displaced employees during boundary realignment is a live test of public employer practices. Council on recess until July 23 (31 DAYS). Bond final vote July 23 — FY2027 budget conversations will include workforce retention and benefit policies.

June 23 update: Research phase continues. AISD boundary workshops concluding — virtual session tonight (6-7:30 PM). AISD approved $205M budget with 558 positions eliminated; impacts public employer landscape. Bond survey closed today. Council on recess until July 23 (30 DAYS).

**June 24 update:** AISD budget approved at $205M — 558 positions eliminated; impacts AISD employees as public employer. Raising Travis County at $51.7M+ distributed. Council on recess until July 23 (29 DAYS). Filing period for council races opens July 19 (25 DAYS).

**June 25 update:** Raising Travis County at $51.7M+ distributed. Bond battle: council majority directs staff to build ~$400M package; staff recommends delaying to 2028. Council on recess until July 23 (28 DAYS). Filing period for council races opens July 19 (24 DAYS).

**June 26 update:** $17.65M childcare expansion approved. Bond: task force proposed up to $766.5M; council majority directed ~$400M. Council on recess until July 23 (27 DAYS). Filing period for council races opens July 19 (23 DAYS).

**June 27 update:** City Manager budget presentation July 16 (19 DAYS). Bond: ~$400M package being developed; housing excluded. Council on recess until July 23 (26 DAYS). Filing period for council races opens July 19 (22 DAYS).
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: Public employers are directly accountable to elected officials. Council members, county commissioners, and school board trustees vote on employee benefit policies and respond to organized constituent pressure. Unlike private employers where the lever is labor market competition, public employers can be moved by showing up at hearings and making specific policy asks. A parental leave improvement at the City of Austin sets a precedent and creates competitive pressure on Travis County and AISD. Wins here are concrete, measurable, and demonstrate the org's ability to produce policy change — which builds credibility for the harder private-sector campaigns.
next_actions: Submit public records requests for current parental leave policies at City of Austin, Travis County, and AISD
Benchmark Austin public employer leave policies against peer cities (Denver, Portland, Minneapolis, Seattle)
Draft specific policy ask: target weeks of paid leave for birth and non-birth parents at each entity
Identify upcoming council, commissioners court, and school board meetings with relevant agenda items
Prepare public comment testimony and recruit 5-10 speakers for first hearing
Build coalition support from city employee associations and unions

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of Parental Leave at City Hall often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Campaigns with published milestones and public reporting earn broader support and allow mid-course corrections.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "If Parental Leave at City Hall is worth doing, it is worth measuring with transparent benchmarks so everyone can see what is working."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Implement change in ways that reduce disruption and share benefits across existing residents and newcomers.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Phased implementation and regular public feedback often lower backlash while improving policy durability.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "We can move Parental Leave at City Hall forward while still listening to neighborhood-level concerns and adjusting implementation details."

Residents deserve clear communication about tradeoffs.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin YIMBY Action
aligned_organizations.relationship: Primary cross-movement ally. Their housing supply advocacy directly enables family formation. Our family-sized housing campaign feeds into their development advocacy. Shared interest in council elections and golf course rezoning.

aligned_organizations.name: AURA
aligned_organizations.relationship: Aligned on housing supply and affordability. Their policy expertise informs our family housing advocacy.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Tech Alliance
aligned_organizations.relationship: Potential partner for employer index distribution and family-friendly workplace advocacy. Tech employer network.

aligned_organizations.name: United Way for Greater Austin
aligned_organizations.relationship: Aligned on childcare access and family economic stability. Potential data sharing partner for childcare mapping.

aligned_organizations.name: Workforce Solutions Capital Area
aligned_organizations.relationship: Administers childcare subsidy programs. Key information source for understanding childcare access barriers.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin ISD
aligned_organizations.relationship: Shared interest in keeping families in urban Austin. School quality and enrollment data inform housing advocacy.

aligned_organizations.name: Any Baby Can
aligned_organizations.relationship: Austin-based family support nonprofit. Aligned on early childhood and family services infrastructure.

aligned_organizations.name: AFSCME Local 1624
aligned_organizations.relationship: City of Austin employee union. Natural ally for parental leave advocacy at City Hall. Their members directly benefit from improved leave policies.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Safe & Sound
aligned_organizations.relationship: Cross-movement ally. Family instability is a major pathway into homelessness. Our family formation infrastructure work — childcare, parental support, family housing — prevents the crises that lead to people losing housing. Their accountability work on homelessness services ensures the safety net works for families in crisis.

aligned_organizations.name: OC Abundance Project
aligned_organizations.relationship: Cross-location ally. Same mission, different context. OC faces even higher housing costs ($1.2M-$1.6M median) and benefits from California's stronger state framework. Share research methodology, employer index design, and childcare desert mapping approach.

engagement_pipeline.awareness: Social media, tech community forums, parenting groups, childcare provider networks. Publish the employer index and childcare desert map for organic distribution. Share the comparison of Austin public employer parental leave policies vs. peer cities.
engagement_pipeline.interest: Attend a family policy briefing. Read the employer index. Check if your neighborhood is a childcare desert. Share your own family formation story.
engagement_pipeline.action: Submit public comment on childcare zoning reform. Share the employer index with your HR department. Attend a county commissioners meeting on Raising Travis County fund deployment. Testify at planning commission on family-sized housing. Testify at city council or commissioners court for improved public employee parental leave.
engagement_pipeline.leadership: Join the research team for the employer index. Lead the childcare mapping data analysis. Coordinate with Austin YIMBY Action on joint housing campaigns. Represent us at county budget hearings. Lead the public employer leave policy research and benchmarking effort.

immediate.action: Track HB 500 implementation: monitor TX Workforce Commission for how $100M in childcare scholarships will be distributed — identify Austin/Travis County allocation

Track AISD recommended budget presentation to Board TODAY (MAY 21) — 215 FTE positions cut, larger class sizes confirmed. Final board vote June 18 (28 DAYS). Directly affects family retention in urban Austin
Send employer survey for family-friendly index — Austin's top 25 tech employers
Compile public records responses on parental leave policies — City of Austin, Travis County, AISD

medium_term.action: Complete and publish childcare desert map v1 — include overlay with new HB 500 scholarship eligibility zones
Draft childcare zoning reform proposal for residential areas
Publish comparison of Austin public employer parental leave policies vs. peer city benchmarks
Submit comments on $766.5M bond package — advocate for family-oriented public facilities in the $131M public facilities allocation

strategic.action: Publish Austin Family-Friendly Employer Index v1
strategic.notes: Timed to get media coverage before November elections — makes family policy part of the council race conversation.

strategic.action: Advocate for family-sized housing incentives in DBC density bonus program and golf course rezoning
strategic.notes: The DBC public hearing is TODAY (May 21). Advocate for 2BR+ family-sized unit requirements in the density bonus framework. ⚡ CRITICAL: The $353M parks-only bond counter-proposal (May 20) excludes the $200M housing allocation — advocate for the full $766.5M BEATF package through Austin YIMBY Action coalition.

strategic.action: Present combined Raising Travis County + HB 500 accountability report to commissioners and state legislators
strategic.notes: Track the $5M county investment AND the new $100M state scholarship program at facility and neighborhood level. The combined city-county-state childcare investment is unprecedented — rigorous accountability reporting establishes the org's credibility and ensures funds reach childcare deserts.

strategic.action: Advocate for specific parental leave policy improvements at City of Austin council and Travis County Commissioners Court
strategic.notes: Specific ask: 12 weeks paid leave for birth parents, 6 weeks for non-birth parents at all three public employers. Concrete, measurable policy win that demonstrates org effectiveness.

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Families face real anxiety that "abundance" language can overlook neighborhood character, school crowding, and whether new housing and employer benefits will actually reach middle-income households instead of primarily helping higher earners.

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Abundance means reducing the "family penalty" in housing, childcare, and time.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Family-sized homes and childcare access are strongly tied to labor-force participation and household stability.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "Our goal is simple: make it possible for families to have kids and stay in Austin without being priced out."

cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Employers with family-friendly policies and nearby attainable housing see better retention and lower replacement costs.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "If companies want a stable workforce, they need workers who can afford housing and childcare close to work."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Intergenerational stability and care work are shared moral priorities.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Communities with accessible housing and childcare support stronger social networks and civic participation.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "This is pro-family in the most practical sense: homes, childcare, and time together."

common_ground: Kids should be able to grow up near their support networks.
Work should support family life, not crowd it out.
Policy should be judged by real household outcomes.
conversation_traps_to_avoid: Avoid implying that renters and homeowners are on opposite teams.
Avoid culture-war framing around family choices.
Avoid treating employer scorecards as "gotcha" tools rather than improvement tools.
when_to_acknowledge_uncertainty: How quickly childcare capacity can scale by neighborhood.
How much local reforms alone can offset statewide cost pressures.
Which employer policy combinations drive the biggest retention gains.
honest_limits: We are not a direct childcare provider.
We cannot by ourselves change state or federal tax policy.
We cannot promise every employer will adopt best practices immediately.
